title: Europe
subtitle: Post-Roman — illuminated manuscripts to post-impressionism.
intro: A thousand years of churches, altarpieces, and — eventually — secular portraits, landscapes, and the individual human strangeness at the turn of the 20th century.

works.title: Book of Kells
works.desc: A Latin gospel book from monks on Iona and Kells. The ornament is so intricate that Gerald of Wales thought it "the work of an angel, not of a man."

works.title: Chartres Cathedral
works.desc: Rebuilt in 26 years after a fire. Its stained glass — especially the "Chartres blue" — has never been matched; the recipe was lost.

works.title: The Arnolfini Portrait
works.desc: A merchant and his wife in a Bruges interior. The convex mirror on the back wall reflects two more figures — one of them possibly the painter.

works.title: The School of Athens
works.desc: Plato and Aristotle at the center, surrounded by the Greek philosophical tradition. Raphael slipped portraits of himself and Michelangelo into the crowd.

works.title: David
works.desc: Seventeen feet of marble carved from a block others had given up on. David faces Goliath before the stone flies — tense, not triumphant.

works.title: Sistine Chapel Ceiling
works.desc: Four years, mostly alone, on scaffolding. Michelangelo considered himself a sculptor, not a painter, and complained about the job the whole way through.

works.title: The Night Watch
works.desc: A militia portrait transformed into a theatrical scene — figures stepping forward, a drumbeat, a girl glowing in the crowd. Not actually set at night.

works.title: Las Meninas
works.desc: The Infanta and her attendants — but who is the painting for? The king and queen appear only in the mirror. Foucault wrote an essay on that mirror.

works.title: Girl with a Pearl Earring
works.desc: Not a portrait but a "tronie" — a study of an imagined figure. The pearl itself is two quick strokes of white paint. We don't know who she was.

works.title: The Starry Night
works.desc: Painted from the window of his asylum room at Saint-Rémy, from memory. The village below is imagined; the swirling sky he said he could actually see.

works.title: The Scream
works.desc: Munch wrote in his diary: "I sensed an infinite scream passing through nature." The sky's red may be from the Krakatoa eruption ten years earlier.

works.title: Bayeux Tapestry
works.desc: Seventy meters of embroidered linen telling the Norman conquest of England. Probably stitched by English nuns for the new conqueror's half-brother.

works.title: Ghent Altarpiece
works.meta: Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1432 · Belgium
works.desc: Twelve folding panels of unmatched detail. Stolen seven times across its history; the Just Judges panel has been missing since 1934.

works.title: The Birth of Venus
works.desc: Venus rises from the sea on a shell, blown to shore by wind-gods. Painted on canvas when fresco was still the fashion — and kept hidden from Savonarola's fires.

works.title: The Last Supper
works.desc: Painted on a refectory wall in an experimental technique that began flaking within decades. The fragility is part of the legend.

works.title: The Garden of Earthly Delights
works.desc: A triptych of Eden, excess, and hell. The right panel is crowded with monsters nobody has fully decoded — including one with sheet music tattooed on a buttock.

works.title: Impression, Sunrise
works.desc: The painting that gave a movement its name — a hostile critic meant "impression" as an insult. Hazy harbor, red sun, no detail that isn't a brushstroke.

works.title: The Kiss
works.desc: Gold leaf and oil on canvas. A couple fused inside a shimmering shroud of pattern — Klimt's answer to the new century.

works.title: Notre-Dame de Paris
works.desc: Cathedral of the kings of France. Half the fabric is original medieval, half is Viollet-le-Duc's 19th-century reimagining. The 2019 fire took the spire.

works.title: The Isenheim Altarpiece
works.desc: Painted for a hospital for plague and ergotism. Christ's body on the cross is covered in exactly the sores the patients saw on their own.

works.title: Primavera
works.desc: Venus in a grove of citrus. The three Graces, Mercury, and the story of Chloris turning into Flora — a Medici wedding gift, decoded differently in every generation.

works.title: Pietà
works.desc: Carved by a 24-year-old. Mary is impossibly young — younger than her son — because, Michelangelo said, virtue preserves her. His only signed work.

works.title: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
works.desc: Pointillism's founding canvas — ten feet wide, built entirely from tiny dots. Parisians on a Sunday afternoon, rendered like nothing before.

works.title: Water Lilies
works.desc: Two hundred and fifty paintings of the pond in his garden, late in life, eyes failing. The largest canvases wrap around the room at the Orangerie.

works.title: The Thinker
works.desc: Originally a small figure above The Gates of Hell — Dante, looking down at his Inferno. Enlarged and cast on its own, it became a universal emblem of thought.

works.title: Wanderer above the Sea of Fog
works.desc: A man stands on a rocky peak, his back to us, looking into swirling cloud. Romanticism in one image — the sublime, and the lone self against it.

works.title: The Third of May 1808
works.desc: A Madrid citizen, arms flung wide, faces a French firing squad in lantern-light. Painted as an accusation — art reporting on atrocity for the first time.

works.title: The Hay Wain
works.desc: A farm cart in a shallow stream on a summer afternoon. Nothing is happening. Constable turned rural quiet into monumental painting.

works.title: Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe
works.desc: A nude woman picnics with two fully dressed men. The scandal wasn't the nudity — it was that she looks directly at the viewer, unashamed.

works.title: Olympia
works.desc: A courtesan reclining nude, a Black maid bringing flowers, a cat raising its tail. The Salon hung it high to keep people from slashing it.

works.title: Melencolia I
works.desc: A winged figure sits surrounded by tools — compass, scales, hourglass — unable to act. Dürer's engraving of creative despair, loaded with symbols art historians still argue about.

works.title: The Calling of Saint Matthew
works.desc: Christ points across a shadowed tavern. A shaft of light slides down the back wall. Matthew gestures at himself — "me?" — caravaggio inventing a whole new visual language.

works.title: The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp
works.desc: A public dissection of an executed criminal, witnessed by seven guild surgeons. Rembrandt was 26; it made his reputation.

works.title: The Milkmaid
works.desc: A kitchen maid pours milk into a bowl. Morning light from the window. The whole painting is about the thin stream of milk and the pause it creates.

works.title: Hunters in the Snow
works.desc: Three hunters returning empty-handed to a frozen village; dogs slink behind them; skaters on the far ponds. One of the most lived-in winter scenes ever painted.

works.title: Liberty Leading the People
works.desc: Painted after the July Revolution that overthrew Charles X. Liberty strides over the barricades, bare-breasted, bayonet and tricolor raised. The boy with pistols later became Hugo's Gavroche.

works.title: The Raft of the Medusa
works.desc: A real recent disaster: 147 men cut loose on a raft off Senegal after the captain took the lifeboats. Fifteen survived. Géricault visited morgues and built a model in his studio.

works.title: The Coronation of Napoleon
works.desc: Nearly ten meters wide. Napoleon, having just placed the crown on his own head, is about to crown Joséphine. David inserted Napoleon's mother in the grandstand though she refused to attend.

works.title: The Wedding at Cana
works.desc: The largest painting in the Louvre at seventy square meters. Painted for a Venetian refectory; Napoleon's troops cut it in half in 1797 to ship it to France.

works.title: The Lacemaker
works.meta: Dutch Golden Age · c. 1669–1671 · Louvre, Paris
works.desc: Only ten inches tall. A girl bent over her bobbins in concentrated silence — Vermeer's smallest canvas and one of his most intense studies of attention.

works.title: Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss
works.desc: Canova's marble of the moment Cupid wakes Psyche with a kiss. Designed to be circled — every angle offers a different composition.

works.title: La Grande Odalisque
works.desc: A concubine reclines on impossibly long, boneless anatomy. Critics counted extra vertebrae; Ingres was not interested in bones — he was interested in line.

works.title: Scrovegni Chapel Frescoes
works.desc: Giotto's cycle of the life of the Virgin and Christ. A man paid for it to save his father from hell — his father appears in the painting, handing over the chapel model.

works.title: The Fighting Temeraire
works.desc: A storied warship, veteran of Trafalgar, tugged by a smudgy little steam tug to the breaker's yard. Turner's elegy for the age of sail — voted Britain's favorite painting.

works.title: Judith Beheading Holofernes
works.desc: Judith, arms extended to keep the blood off her dress, saws through the Assyrian general's neck. Caravaggio's first great biblical violence — the beginning of his reputation.

works.title: Sunflowers
works.desc: Painted in Arles to decorate Gauguin's guest bedroom. Van Gogh made seven versions; in letters, he called them an ode to gratitude.

works.title: View of Delft
works.desc: Vermeer's hometown across the water, clouds moving overhead. Proust called it "the most beautiful painting in the world" and had one of his characters die looking at it.

works.title: The Treachery of Images
works.desc: A pipe painted with the words "Ceci n'est pas une pipe" (This is not a pipe). It isn't — it's a painting of one. Magritte's founding joke of Surrealism.

works.title: Mona Lisa (La Joconde)
works.meta: c. 1503–1519 · Oil on poplar · Salle des États
works.desc: Probably a portrait of Lisa Gherardini, wife of a Florentine merchant. Leonardo carried it with him for years, reworking it endlessly, and it never left his side until his death in France. Her fame owes as much to theft as to genius: when Vincenzo Peruggia stole her in 1911 and kept her in a Paris apartment for two years, the empty wall drew larger crowds than the painting ever had.

works.title: The Cheat with the Ace of Diamonds
works.meta: c. 1636–1638 · Oil on canvas · Sully Wing
works.desc: Four figures, four glances, one con. A naïve young nobleman is about to lose his fortune to the cheat on the left, who has pulled an ace from his belt. The courtesan and her servant are in on it; only the young man isn't looking at anyone.

works.title: The Dying Slave
works.desc: Intended for the tomb of Pope Julius II, a project that consumed Michelangelo for forty years and was never finished as planned. The figure is not dying but — perhaps — falling asleep, or surrendering to ecstasy. Michelangelo left the back deliberately rough.

works.title: The Marly Horses
works.desc: Commissioned for the royal estate of Marly, where Louis XV wanted rearing horses at the entrance to his watering pond. Each is over 3.5 meters tall. They stood outdoors for nearly two centuries before being brought inside in 1984.

works.title: Crown of Louis XV
works.meta: 1722 · Silver gilt, originally set with the crown jewels · Galerie d'Apollon
works.desc: Made for the coronation of the twelve-year-old Louis XV at Reims. The original diamonds — including the Régent, now displayed nearby — were removed after the ceremony and replaced with paste copies, which are what you see today.

works.title: The Napoleon III Apartments
works.desc: Not a single object but an entire suite of state rooms — crimson silk, gilded stucco, chandeliers heavy enough to frighten the ceiling. These were the ceremonial apartments of the Minister of State under the Second Empire. Walk in and the museum disappears.

works.title: The Régent Diamond
works.desc: Found in India around 1698, smuggled by a slave who hid it in a wound in his leg, sold to Thomas Pitt (grandfather of William Pitt the Elder), and eventually bought by the French crown. Worn by Louis XV, Louis XVI, and set into Napoleon's sword. Still considered the most perfectly cut historical diamond in the world.

works.title: Drawings by Leonardo da Vinci
works.meta: c. 1480–1510 · Chalk, ink, and silverpoint on paper
works.desc: The Louvre holds over twenty sheets by Leonardo — studies of hands, horses, the tilt of a woman's head, the flow of water. A single silverpoint line, set down and never corrected.

works.title: Knight, Death and the Devil
works.desc: One of Dürer's three "Master Engravings." A Christian knight rides impassively through a desolate gorge, unmoved by the hourglass-bearing Death beside him or the goat-headed Devil behind. The technical summit of Renaissance printmaking.

works.title: Self-Portraits by Rembrandt
works.desc: Rembrandt made over forty etched self-portraits across his lifetime. The Louvre holds a remarkable set. In ink on paper he is looser and more playful than in his painted selves — pulling faces, squinting, trying on hats.

works.title: Composition VII
works.desc: Kandinsky called it the most complex work he ever painted — over thirty preparatory studies. Motifs of resurrection, flood, and last judgment dissolve into color and line.

works.title: Composition with Red, Blue and Yellow
works.desc: Black lines, three primary colors, white negative space, canvas flat as the grid itself. Mondrian believed he had found the underlying logic of visible reality.

works.title: I and the Village
works.desc: A green-faced peasant and a white goat exchange a look across a village that floats and spins. Chagall's memory of his hometown of Vitebsk, filtered through Paris.

works.title: The Death of Marat
works.desc: Marat in his bathtub, stabbed by Charlotte Corday, pen still in hand. David painted him as a secular martyr — a revolutionary Pietà for a Republic that had killed its king.

works.title: Les Demoiselles d'Avignon
works.desc: Five Barcelona prostitutes, faces half-African mask, bodies fractured into angular planes. Picasso hid it for years; when it emerged it had already ended the 19th century.

works.title: Cologne Cathedral
works.desc: Started in 1248, stopped in 1473, resumed in 1842, finished with the original medieval drawings in hand. For four years after completion it was the tallest building on earth.

works.title: Florence Cathedral (Duomo)
works.desc: Brunelleschi's dome, still the largest brick dome ever built. He designed it without drawings, built it without scaffolding, and took the method with him to the grave.

works.title: Palace of Versailles
works.desc: Louis XIV's hunting lodge turned seat of French monarchy. The Hall of Mirrors reflects the west-facing garden at sunset; every piece of furniture was once pure silver.

works.title: Santiago de Compostela Cathedral
works.desc: The end of the Camino de Santiago — pilgrims walk 800 kilometers to this high altar and the silver tomb of St. James. The Botafumeiro swings eighty feet across the transept at mass.

works.title: Saturn Devouring His Son
works.desc: Goya painted it on the wall of his dining room. Old, deaf, sick, he let Saturn eat his son with hollow eyes. Transferred to canvas after his death; not meant for anyone to see.

works.title: Sistine Madonna
works.desc: Mary descending on clouds; two bored cherubs resting their chins on the painting's lower frame. Those cherubs — cropped, enlarged, spun off — became one of the most reproduced images of any era.

works.title: Café Terrace at Night
works.desc: The yellow terrace still stands in Arles (renamed after the painting). No black in the night sky — deep cobalt blue everywhere instead. Van Gogh's first major work done at night.

works.title: The Potato Eaters
works.desc: Dark, muddy, deliberately ugly. Van Gogh wrote that he wanted to show hands that had dug the earth eating the potatoes those hands had grown. His breakthrough, though critics hated it.

works.title: The Ambassadors
works.desc: Two French diplomats stand beside a table of instruments — lutes, globes, sundials. At the bottom, a distorted smear that becomes a human skull when you look from the side. A memento mori in code.

works.title: Self-Portrait as the Allegory of Painting
works.desc: Artemisia paints herself in the act of painting — arms muscled, sleeves pushed up. She was the first woman admitted to the Accademia di Belle Arti di Firenze.

works.title: Siena Cathedral
works.desc: Striped in black and white marble. The floor is inlaid with 56 narrative panels — the largest program of marble intarsia in Italy, covered most of the year to protect it.

works.title: St. Peter's Basilica
works.desc: Built over the tomb of Peter. Bramante started it, Michelangelo finished the dome, Bernini added the piazza's embracing colonnade — the collective monument of Renaissance Rome.

works.title: Byzantine Mosaics of Hagia Sophia
works.desc: Figural mosaics on gold ground. The Virgin and Child in the apse, unveiled in 867 to reverse the Iconoclasm, glows from half a kilometer away.

works.title: Pala d'Oro
works.desc: The golden altarpiece of San Marco. 250 enameled panels, 1,927 gems, looted from Constantinople in 1204 and then added to across four centuries. Still in daily use.

works.title: Rose Window
works.desc: A massive tracery wheel of stained glass — the Gothic cathedral's visual sermon, laying out salvation in colored light. Chartres' rose blue was never surpassed; Strasbourg's west rose is a rival.

works.title: Saint Sophia Cathedral, Kyiv
works.desc: A Byzantine five-nave cathedral at the heart of Kyivan Rus. Its mosaics of the Virgin Orans ("Indestructible Wall") are said to have protected the city through a thousand years of war.

works.title: Bacchus
works.desc: Caravaggio's boyish, tipsy Bacchus — fingernails dirty, a decaying fruit basket in the foreground. Lost for three centuries, rediscovered in a Uffizi storeroom in 1913.

works.title: The Gleaners
works.desc: Three peasant women stoop to pick up leftover grain after the harvest. The wealthy hated it — it looked like socialism. Millet painted rural labor as dignified, not picturesque.

works.title: Westminster Abbey
works.desc: Coronation church of English monarchs since 1066; also a vast, crowded graveyard — Newton, Darwin, Stephen Hawking. Poets' Corner adds Chaucer, Dickens, T. S. Eliot.

works.title: Reims Cathedral
works.desc: Coronation church of the kings of France. Shelled for five years in World War I; its sculptures, including the famous Smiling Angel, shattered and then were painstakingly reassembled.

works.title: The Dance
works.desc: Five red figures hold hands in a frenzied ring against blue sky and green earth. Matisse called the composition "the highest point of the story" — the first great Modernist painting of pure color.

works.title: The Ecstasy of Saint Teresa
works.meta: Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1652 · Cornaro Chapel, Rome
works.desc: The Carmelite mystic swoons in midair as a smiling angel lifts a gold-tipped arrow above her heart. Bernini turned the entire chapel into a theater: the Cornaro patrons watch from opera-box side panels, a hidden window rains gilded light down on the marble.

works.title: Apollo and Daphne
works.desc: Bernini caught the instant Daphne's fingers sprout leaves and her feet root into bark, her scream frozen just as Apollo's hand grazes her ribcage. He was twenty-six when he finished it and already the most technically audacious sculptor in Rome.

works.title: The Rape of Proserpina
works.desc: Pluto's fingertips sink visibly into Proserpina's thigh — marble impersonating flesh. Bernini was twenty-three; the group stunned Rome into realizing that Baroque sculpture had outrun the limits of Hellenistic virtuosity.

works.title: Fountain of the Four Rivers
works.meta: Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1651 · Piazza Navona, Rome
works.desc: Allegories of the Nile, Ganges, Danube, and Río de la Plata lounge around a hollowed travertine crag supporting an Egyptian obelisk. The Nile covers his face because, according to legend, the source of the river was still unknown — or because he can't bear to look at Borromini's church opposite.

works.title: The Death of Socrates
works.meta: Jacques-Louis David · 1787 · Metropolitan Museum of Art
works.desc: Socrates reaches for the hemlock while lecturing his grieving disciples on immortality, one finger stabbing heavenward. David painted it on the eve of the Revolution; Reynolds called it the greatest modern history painting.

works.title: Oath of the Horatii
works.desc: Three brothers swear on their father's swords to die for Rome while their women collapse in a curve of grief. Commissioned by Louis XVI and unveiled five years before the Revolution, the painting defined Neoclassical moral rigor for a generation.

works.title: Napoleon Crossing the Alps
works.desc: David invented the cinematic pose — rearing horse, windswept cape, outstretched finger — that the actual crossing lacked. Napoleon, who had used a mule, told David to paint him 'calm on a fiery horse' and handed him five replicas to produce.

works.title: The Swing
works.desc: A young woman kicks off her slipper while her hidden lover peeks up her billowing dress — the jilted bishop pushing the swing behind her. The Baron de Saint-Julien commissioned it explicitly to stage this visual joke at his mistress's expense.

works.title: Pilgrimage to Cythera
works.desc: Watteau's reception piece for the Académie: couples linger and depart from the island of love, a gilded barge waiting, cupids swirling overhead. The genre he invented — the fête galante — gave eighteenth-century painting its register of wistful pleasure.

works.title: The Garden of Love
works.meta: Peter Paul Rubens · 1633 · Museo del Prado
works.desc: A riotous outdoor party Rubens painted after his second marriage — his young wife Hélène Fourment appears three times among the guests. Cupids tug couples together while a fountain with the Three Graces soaks the scene in divinely sanctioned pleasure.

works.title: Samson and Delilah (Rubens)
works.meta: Peter Paul Rubens · 1610 · National Gallery, London
works.desc: Delilah watches tenderly while a Philistine crops Samson's hair as he sleeps on her lap. The candlelit nocturne and coiled composition mark Rubens's first mature statement after returning from Italy, still vibrating with Caravaggio.

works.title: Dance at Le Moulin de la Galette
works.desc: A Sunday afternoon at the Montmartre dance hall, sunlight filtering through acacia leaves onto the dancers in dapples Renoir invented a new vocabulary of color to paint. His friends posed for it over several months.

works.title: Luncheon of the Boating Party
works.desc: Renoir's friends — among them his future wife Aline, caressing a little dog — laze on a restaurant terrace overlooking the Seine. It is impressionism at its most convivial: no protest, no weather, just a warm afternoon extended indefinitely.

works.title: The Sleeping Gypsy
works.meta: Henri Rousseau · 1897 · Museum of Modern Art
works.desc: A dark-robed traveler sleeps beside her mandolin while a lion sniffs her face under a flat full moon. Rousseau, a self-taught customs clerk, offered to sell it to his hometown mayor for 200 francs; he was politely declined.

works.title: The Dream (Rousseau)
works.meta: Henri Rousseau · 1910 · Museum of Modern Art
works.desc: Rousseau's final completed work: a nude Yadwigha reclines on a Second Empire sofa that floats through a moonlit jungle of invented foliage, a snake charmer piping behind. He insisted the sofa's incongruity was perfectly explained by the word 'dream.'

works.title: The Red Studio
works.meta: Henri Matisse · 1911 · Museum of Modern Art
works.desc: Matisse soaked his Issy studio in uniform Venetian red, leaving his own paintings as the only color notes on the wall. He had begun with green and blue underneath; the radical decision to cover everything in a single pigment recast what a painting could be.

works.title: Senecio
works.desc: A geometrically segmented face painted in ochres and pinks, the eyes slightly mismatched so it never quite looks back. Klee claimed the title 'Old Man' (Senecio in Latin); the image belongs somewhere between clown, sun god, and child's drawing.

works.title: The Elephant Celebes
works.desc: A giant metallic elephant based on a photograph of a Sudanese corn bin looms over a headless mannequin beside a mysterious fish-stalk. Ernst's breakthrough Surrealist work, painted before André Breton had even coined the term.

works.title: Broadway Boogie Woogie
works.meta: Piet Mondrian · 1943 · Museum of Modern Art
works.desc: Mondrian's last completed painting, made after he fled wartime Europe for New York. The jittering yellow grid mimics Manhattan's street plan and the jazz he had fallen in love with; black bars are banished for the first time in decades.

works.title: Nighthawks
works.meta: Edward Hopper · 1942 · Art Institute of Chicago
works.desc: Four figures in a late-night Greenwich Avenue diner, insulated by its curved glass from the empty street outside. Painted weeks after Pearl Harbor, it fixed the look of twentieth-century American loneliness.

works.title: American Gothic
works.meta: Grant Wood · 1930 · Art Institute of Chicago
works.desc: Wood's sister Nan and his dentist Byron McKeeby posed as a farmer and his daughter in front of a white-board Iowa house. Meant by Wood as a gentle tribute, the image has been parodied perhaps more than any other American painting.

works.title: Vertumnus
works.desc: Portrait of Emperor Rudolf II as Vertumnus, god of seasons — his face built from pears, grapes, squash, grain. Arcimboldo's joke on Habsburg dignity; the emperor loved it.

works.title: The Cardsharps
works.desc: An early Caravaggio: a boy is cheated at cards by a pair of accomplices — one palming a card behind his back, the other reading his hand over his shoulder. A career-making genre scene.

works.title: The Milkmaid of Bordeaux
works.desc: Goya's last canvas, painted at 81 in exile in Bordeaux. A young woman looking down over her shoulder — loose, almost proto-Impressionist brushwork. Bequeathed to his last companion.
